channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: IOI Partners no longer wants anything to do with MindsEye
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

March 18, 2026

1 Min Read

Image via Build A Rocket Boy

IOI Partners has stepped down as the publisher of MindsEye, the critically panned title from Build A Rocket Boy (BARB). 

The Danish publisher—a division of Hitman developer IO Interactive—concluded its collaboration with MindsEye on March 16, 2026. BARB will now assume sole publishing responsibilities for the title. 

"IOI Partners' involvement with MindsEye comes to an end, except for any essential transitional functions required to transfer publisher-of-record status to Build A Rocket Boy," reads a press release. 

"Build A Rocket Boy will assume sole publishing responsibilities going forward, ensuring continuity for the MindsEye community and all partners. IOI Partners and BARB are coordinating closely to ensure a seamless transition over the coming weeks."

Notably, the separation means a planned Hitman crossover event within MindsEye will no longer take place. BARB said it still intends on collaborating with other partners in the future.

Related:Ubisoft names new Creative House and Creative Network leaders

Whether those plans come to fruition remains to be seen. The UK studio, which was established by former Rockstar North president Leslie Benzies, is currently in disarray after making widespread layoffs following the launch of MindsEye while also claiming it has fallen victim to 'corporate sabotage.'

Two key BARB executives also departed the studio one week before MindsEye hit shelves. Following the launch of MindsEye, a number of current and former BARB workers signed an open letter to surface allegations of mismanagement and crunch. 

The letter, published with assistance from the Game Workers Branch of the Independent Workers of Great Britain union, said that between 250 to 300 workers had been subject to "longstanding disrespect and mistreatment" during their time at the studio. 

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/production/ioi-partners-no-longer-wants-anything-to-do-with-mindseye
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Remedy releases final major update for underperforming shooter FBC: Firebreak
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

March 18, 2026

2 Min Read

FBC: Firebreak was meant to be a proof-of-concept: a genre-bending Control spinoff that swapped story-driven surrealism for cooperative multiplayer antics. It was also, notably, Remedy's first stab at self-publishing.

Yet, after less than a year on shelves, Remedy has confirmed it will no longer be releasing major content updates for FBC: Firebreak. 

The Finnish studio said it will ensure the title remains online and playable for "years to come," and explained engineering work has been completed to ensure relay server structure can be sustainably maintained even with lower player volumes. 

A permanent price drop has also been issued to make the game more accessible to new players. As of today, the base game will cost $19.99 while the deluxe edition will retail for $29.99. 

The final update, dubbed 'Open House,' introduces brand new Endless Shift arenas inspired by Control gameplay improvements and balance updates. It also includes a new 'Friend's Pass' feature that allows players to invite those who don't own the game to their sessions. 

Related:Games for Change research director unveils training program for community-facing professionals

Remedy lauds FBC: Firebreak development team for their 'passion and experimentation'

FBC: Firebreak launched on June 17, 2025. By July, Remedy said the title was underperforming on a commercial level, but claimed it had "succeeded technically" after reaching over 1 million players in 10 days. 

Many of those players, however, didn't stick around for the long haul.

"The game's initial onboarding experience and mission structure resulted in high early player drop-offs and an influx of negative reviews. As players spent more time in the game and we released updates improving the game, sentiment in reviews turned more positive," said former Remedy CEO, Tero Virtala, at the time. 

"Commercially, we were unsatisfied with the launch-phase consumer sales of FBC: Firebreak. Thus far, FBC: Firebreak’s commercial performance has largely been driven by the Xbox and PlayStation subscription service agreements."

A few months later, Remedy issued a profit warning with FBC: Firebreak still struggling to make an impact. Virtala departed shortly after and was replaced by interim CEO Markus Mäki, who attempted to instil a sense of urgency in a bid to turn around the studio's fortunes. 

Earlier this year, Remedy named former EA executive Jean-Charles Gaudechon as its new (permanent) CEO. Gaudechon said he intends to scale the company in a way that "builds lasting value."

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/remedy-releases-final-major-update-for-underperforming-shooter-fbc-firebreak
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Ubisoft names new Creative House and Creative Network leaders
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

March 18, 2026

2 Min Read

Ubisoft has announced more leadership appointments to support the implementation of its new Creative House operating model.

The Assassin's Creed publisher has named former Tencent Games Global head of development, Julien Bares, as the general manager of Creative Houses 3 and 5.

For context, Creative House 3 has been tasked with overseeing live experiences such as For Honor, The Crew, Riders Republic, Brawlhalla, and Skull & Bones. 

Creative House 5 will handle the company's casual brands including Just Dance and Idle Miner Tycoon.

Prior to his recent stint at Tencent, Bares served as general manager of 2K China. Notably, he began his career at Ubisoft, initially working as a producer on Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon and Tom Clancy's Splinter Cell. 

"I entered the games industry to create experiences that resonate with players," said Bares. "Leading Creative Houses 3 and 5 is an opportunity to partner with incredibly talented teams to build innovative games and deepen the connections we have with our communities. By combining powerful technology and smart collaboration across our studios, we can push the boundaries and continue building on Ubisoft’s unique creative culture."

Related:Direct-join union UVW-CWA now has 550 members

Ubisoft says new structure will more effectively "support the development of ambitious games"

Ubisoft has also named Massive Entertainment managing director Thomas Andrén as the general manager of its Creative Network, a new studio-wide organisation formed to provide cross-functional creative experience and production capacity to all five of its Creative Houses.

Andrén has been plying his trade at Ubisoft Massive in Stockholm, where he contributed to the launches of Star Wars Outlaws and its DLCs, Avatar: Frontiers of Pandora and the recent From the Ashes DLC—all while helping secure the long-term roadmap for The Division franchise.

He is also credited with strengthening cross-studio collaboration across projects and technology teams by working with internal studios including Ubisoft Bucharest, Ubisoft Craiova and Ubisoft RedLynx. Andrén will step into his new role on April 1, 2026. He will also serve as interim managing director at Massive Entertainment until a successor is appointed.

"The Creative Network within Ubisoft's new operating model represents an incredible concentration of expertise, craftsmanship, and innovation," said Andrén. 

"Each studio brings its own unique strengths and capabilities, and by strengthening collaboration across the network and with our Creative Houses, we can combine agility with world-class expertise to support the development of ambitious games and deliver great new experiences for players."

Related:Unionized game workers launch petition to protect immigrants from ICE agents

Ubisoft's mammoth restructuring comes after widespread studio closures and project cancellations at the French publisher. 

The company expects to make another €200 million in cuts over the next two years. That's despite Tencent investing around €1.16 billion into Vantage Studios (Creative House 1), Ubisoft's new custodian of major franchises like Assassin's Creed and Far Cry.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/production/ubisoft-names-new-creative-house-and-creative-network-leaders
